questions.question: Explain the difference between constructor and destructor in C++ with an example code snippet.
keyPoints: Constructor initializes objects, has same name as class, can be overloaded
Destructor releases resources, has tilde (~) prefix, cannot be overloaded
Constructor called automatically when object created, destructor called when object goes out of scope
Example shows constructor with parameters and destructor with cleanup code

questions.question: Describe the three access specifiers in C++ (public, private, protected) with their visibility rules. Provide an example demonstrating the 'protected' modifier.
keyPoints: Private: accessible only within the class itself
Public: accessible from anywhere in the program
Protected: accessible within class and derived classes but not from outside
Example shows base class with protected members accessed in derived class

questions.question: Explain the 'this' pointer and static data members in C++. How do they differ? Write a C++ class demonstrating both concepts with appropriate example code.
keyPoints: this pointer is an implicit parameter pointing to current object, used to differentiate member variables from parameters
Static members belong to class rather than objects, shared among all instances, accessed using class name
this pointer is per-object, static is per-class
Example code demonstrates static counter tracking number of objects and this pointer used in member function

questions.question: What are friend functions in C++? How do they potentially violate the principle of encapsulation? Write a complete C++ program to demonstrate a friend function accessing private data members of a class.
keyPoints: Friend functions are non-member functions granted access to private and protected members using 'friend' keyword
They break encapsulation by allowing external functions to access internal data
Useful for operator overloading and cooperative functions between classes
Example program shows class with private data, friend function declaration, and demonstration of access
